titleEn: Generative AI on the farm: Know your options
titleZh: 农场上的生成式AI：了解你的选择
summaryZh: 本文探讨了生成式人工智能（Generative AI）在农业中的应用潜力，帮助农场主从众多AI工具中做出明智选择。文章介绍了生成式AI的基本概念、在农场中的实际用途（如作物管理、动物健康监测、市场预测等），以及如何评估不同AI解决方案的适用性。强调不要因选择过多而放弃使用AI，而是通过明确需求、试用工具和逐步集成来提升农场运营效率。
translationZh:
不要让过多的选择阻止你在农业运营中使用人工智能。

生成式人工智能（Generative AI）正在迅速改变各行各业，农业也不例外。从作物管理到动物健康监测，AI工具为农场主提供了前所未有的洞察力和自动化能力。然而，面对市场上琳琅满目的AI解决方案，许多农场主感到不知所措，甚至因此放弃了尝试。

**什么是生成式AI？**

生成式AI是一种能够创建新内容（如文本、图像、代码或数据）的人工智能技术。与传统的分析型AI不同，它不仅能识别模式，还能生成新的、有用的输出。在农业中，这意味着AI可以基于历史数据和实时输入，生成个性化的种植建议、疾病预警报告或市场趋势分析。

**生成式AI在农场中的应用**

1. **作物管理**：生成式AI可以分析土壤传感器、气象站和卫星图像的数据，生成精准的灌溉、施肥和病虫害防治方案。例如，AI可以预测未来几天的降雨模式，并自动调整灌溉计划。

2. **动物健康监测**：通过分析来自可穿戴传感器、摄像头和声音记录的数据，生成式AI可以早期检测牲畜的疾病迹象，甚至预测疫情爆发。例如，AI可以分析猪的咳嗽声，判断是否患有呼吸道疾病。

3. **市场预测**：生成式AI可以整合历史价格、供需数据和全球贸易信息，生成未来农产品价格走势的预测，帮助农场主做出更明智的销售决策。

4. **自动化报告**：AI可以自动生成农场运营报告，包括产量分析、成本核算和效率评估，节省大量人工时间。

**如何选择合适的AI工具？**

面对众多选择，农场主可以遵循以下步骤：

- **明确需求**：首先确定你最需要解决的问题是什么——是提高产量、降低成本、还是改善动物福利？
- **评估数据准备**：生成式AI需要大量高质量数据。检查你的农场是否已经收集了足够的数据（如传感器读数、历史记录等）。
- **试用和比较**：许多AI提供商提供免费试用或演示版本。利用这些机会测试不同工具的性能和易用性。
- **考虑集成**：选择能够与你现有农场管理系统（如ERP、CRM或物联网平台）无缝集成的AI工具。
- **关注支持**：确保供应商提供良好的技术支持和培训，尤其是在初期实施阶段。

**不要被选择吓倒**

生成式AI的潜力巨大，但关键在于开始行动。即使从一个小项目开始，比如用AI分析一个田地的数据，也能带来立竿见影的效果。随着经验的积累，你可以逐步扩大AI的应用范围。

记住，AI不是要取代农场主，而是成为你的得力助手。通过合理利用生成式AI，你可以让农场运营更高效、更可持续，同时腾出更多时间专注于战略决策。
featuredReason: 本文为智慧畜牧行业人士提供了生成式AI在农场应用的实用指南，帮助从业者从众多AI工具中做出明智选择，具有较高的实践参考价值。
contentFull: TechnologyManagementNewsGenerative AI on the farm: Know your optionsDon’t let a deluge of choices stop you from using artificial intelligence in your farming operation.Ben Potter,Senior editor,Farm FuturesJune 26, 2026schedule4 Min ReadGenerative AI programs are similar, but there are some nuances to consider.da-kuk/Getty Images Fight. Flight. Freeze.When it comes to generative AI, the typical “fight or flight” responses don’t really apply. But the ever-increasing number of options may give you pause before getting started.From Claude to ChatGPT, Copilot, Deepseek, Grok, Gemini and many more, these artificial intelligence technologies all promise to become game-changers in your day-to-day activities. They probably won’t revolutionize your operation, but they could still prove to be useful tools on your farm.Getting startedWhen you’re ready to dip your toes into the generative AI waters, the good news is you don’t need to fuss over which program in particular to use, according to Yurii Kovalchuk, CEO of Qaltivate.“All of them work,” he said. “They have very good foundational knowledge of how agriculture works.”Looking at three popular subscription-based generative AI programs, here’s what tends to do best at certain tasks, according to the Substack newsletter Artificial Corner:Related:Tech-savvy daughter brings AI tools to family farmFollowing instructions: Claude ProAudio and video analysis: Gemini ProCasual use: ChatGPT PlusSearch and browsing: all threeImage and video generation: ChatGPT Plus and Gemini ProCoding: Claude ProDaily limits: Gemini Pro (300-plus prompts allowed per day)How you use the program will drive good outcomes, Kovalchuk added.“Creating good prompts is 60% to 70% of your success,” he said. “The better prompts you create, the more definitive results you’ll get.”And because many of these programs offer free versions, it doesn’t hurt to check them against each other.“One trick is to use one program to ask a question and then ask a second program to review those results,” Kovalchuk said. “That’s another way to double-check the output. Sometimes these programs generate nice-looking material, but it doesn’t align with reality.”Janice Person, founder and CEO of Grounded Communications, agreed that pushing back against the results will help keep the tech in check.“Chatbots don’t do that crucial thinking unless you’re going back and forth and asking those critical questions,” she said.For example, if you were to ask ChatGPT about purchasing a new combine, it will assume that’s the outcome you want. Therefore, you may want to ask follow-up questions about maintaining existing equipment to see what other advice it may offer.Another important thing to consider is whether to use free services or pay for more premium features. Premium tiers provide a variety of storage options and a larger cap on the number of prompts you can use. More importantly, paying folds in a layer of privacy.Related:When old school meets new tech, next gen leads the way“Farmers need to think about the fact that what they input is not confidential if they’re not paying for it,” Person said. “There are free accounts everywhere, and you can do a lot for free, but that information is then fed into data banks where others may know that information about you. If you pay for a service, you should have more expectation of privacy.”As the adage goes, “If you’re not paying for the product, you are the product.”One more relevant point: If you plan on asking generative AI the same question often over time — for example, brainstorming ways to diversify your income on a semiannual basis — think about how you can store both the prompts and the results. It doesn’t have to be overly complicated, either. Person does a copy-and-paste of her favorite prompts into a spreadsheet so she can reference them later.Will the bubble burst?Generative AI is not without its detractors. Some tech journalists such as Ed Zitron are well known for their lengthy diatribes about how the technology simply is not sustainable in the long term due to how much it costs to operate. Kovalchuk is a bit more confident that AI has some staying power in some capacity, however.“Whether the AI bubble pops or not, I think the tech is going to stick around, so it’s better to start using it and benefit from it,” he said. “It will never substitute farmers or agronomists, but the ones who use AI might substitute the ones who don’t.”Related:This sleeper technology could revolutionize how farmers weed10 ways to use AI right nowOne good prompt deserves another.With generative AI, it’s all about brainstorming and exploring what these technologies have to offer. Here are a handful of ideas to get you headed in the right direction:Generate scouting task lists for agronomists to help them focus on the highest-risk or highest-value fields.Establish benchmarking reports for your fields to guide improvements.Create predictive maintenance schedules for your farm equipment.Formulate operator training instructions for farm machinery to speed up the onboarding process for your employees.Generate farm financial forecasts that test your budget through different scenarios.Create pricing strategy scenarios to better manage your margins.Automate daily task plans for your team to save time and avoid delays.Generate harvest timing recommendations.Plan strategically with a multiyear farm development road map.Generate animal welfare improvement plans for your livestock.About the AuthorBen PotterSenior editor, Farm FuturesSenior Editor Ben Potter brings two decades of professional agricultural communications and journalism experience to Farm Futures. He began working in the industry in the highly specific world of southern row crop production. Since that time, he has expanded his knowledge to cover a broad range of topics relevant to agriculture, including agronomy, machinery, technology, business, marketing, politics and weather. He has won several writing awards from the American Agricultural Editors Association, most recently on two features about drones and farmers who operate distilleries as a side business. contentHtml: Don’t let a deluge of choices stop you from using artificial intelligence in your farming operation.
